content:
Award classifications

Find out how to read and apply classification descriptions in awards.

Employees covered by an award are paid according to their classification.

On this page: Who can use this information | What an award classification is | Award classifications and pay rates | How to work out which award applies | Steps to work out an employee's classification | Working temporarily at a higher classification | Resolving classification issues | Tools and resources | Related information

What an award classification is

Award classifications (also known as levels or grades) are descriptions of roles and types of work. They're usually found towards the end of an award, often in Schedule A.

Classification descriptions often include information about:
- the types of duties an employee may be expected to perform
- whether the employee supervises other employees
- the level of experience or qualifications an employee needs to work at that level

An employee's minimum pay rate is determined by their award classification. Employers need to make sure they've correctly identified each employee's classification.

Award classifications and pay rates

To work out an employee's minimum pay rate, you will need to know: the award that covers the employee, and their classification level.

An employee's classification can change if their role changes or they get a new qualification.

Employers should review their award's classifications when an employee:
- changes job or duties
- takes on more responsibility, such as becoming a supervisor
- completes the maximum introductory period allowed in their award
- completes relevant training
- obtains a new qualification

How to work out which award applies

To work out if an award covers an employee, you should read the coverage clause (usually clause 4) in the award.

High income employees: Awards don't apply to high income employees. A high income employee is an employee who has accepted a written guarantee of annual earnings and is guaranteed to earn an annual amount that is more than the high income threshold.

Steps to work out an employee's classification

Step 1: Find the classification definitions. Awards contain definitions for each classification under the award. You can usually find classification definitions near the end of the award (often in Schedule A), or in another section such as the pay rates section.

Step 2: Check the award's instructions about classifying employees. Awards may contain an explanation or instructions about how to classify employees. These are usually found at the start of the classification definitions.

Step 3: Consider the classification definitions. To find the right classification, make sure you read the definitions in full and consider all the factors together:
- tasks or duties and job titles
- skills and competence
- qualifications and training
- amount of experience (this can include the number of years at a particular classification level)
- amount of supervision required or performed

An employee's classification is determined by: the main purpose of their role and duties, and how these both make up the major and substantial part of the employee's employment.

These factors consider the:
- nature of the work performed by the employee
- circumstances in which the employee performs the work
- time spent performing each of the employee's duties

Tasks or duties: Consider the tasks or duties an employee is expected to do. Classification definitions usually include lists of 'indicative tasks' that someone would normally do at each level.

Job titles: Classification definitions sometimes also include indicative job titles. Lists of indicative job titles in awards aren't exhaustive.

Skills and competence: An employee's level of competency when performing different tasks is also key in some awards to determine classification.

Qualifications and training: Some awards will require an employee to hold certain qualifications or training to be classified at a particular level.

Amount of experience: Some awards consider the amount of experience an employee has in determining their classification level.

Amount of supervision: The level of supervision an employee requires or provides is a factor in some award classifications.

Step 4: Compare similar classifications. When deciding between two or more classifications, compare the similarities and differences.

Step 5: Share the information. Tell the employee what classification has been set for them and explain why.

Working temporarily at a higher classification

If an employee is required to temporarily work at a higher classification, some awards provide for a higher pay rate while performing the higher duties.

Resolving classification issues

If there's a dispute about an employee's classification, check the award and follow the dispute resolution procedure. If the issue can't be resolved internally, contact us for advice or apply to the Fair Work Commission.

Tools and resources
- Pay and Conditions Tool
- List of awards
- Award summaries
- Find my award
